title: Mark 16
subtitle: The Empty Tomb; The Risen Lord; The Great Commission

heb.paragraph: He has risen — ēgerthē: the divine passive. God raised Jesus from the dead. The passive voice is the NT’s consistent grammar for the resurrection: not that Jesus raised himself but that the Father raised the Son (Acts 2:24; Rom 8:11). The resurrection is the Father’s vindication of the Son.

places.name: The tomb
places.coords: Near Golgotha; Joseph of Arimathea’s newly cut rock tomb
places.text: The same tomb where the women watched the burial (15:47) is where they return at dawn. The tomb’s location near Golgotha and the specific large stone make confusion impossible: they are at the right place. The stone is rolled away; the linen lies there; the angel is seated where the body was.

tl.name: Easter Sunday — The Empty Tomb
tl.text: At dawn on the first day of the week the women find the stone rolled away and the tomb empty. He has risen. The angel sends them to Galilee.

refs.note: After I have risen, I will go ahead of you into Galilee — the promise at the Last Supper that the angel’s message fulfils.
The beginning of the good news — the opening of the Gospel that the empty tomb continues, not concludes.
He has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one… he has not hidden his face from him but has listened — the Passion psalm’s ending of vindication that the resurrection enacts.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: "He has risen!" (ēgerthē) — the divine passive declares the resurrection as an act of God. Marcus notes that the passive voice is the consistent NT grammar for the resurrection: not that Jesus raised himself but that the Father raised the Son (Acts 2:24; 3:15; Rom 8:11; Gal 1:1). The angel's announcement combines identification ("Jesus the Nazarene, who was crucified") with proclamation ("He has risen") — the risen one is the crucified one. The resurrection does not replace the Passion; it vindicates it.

Marcus: "Tell his disciples and Peter" — the specific naming of Peter in the resurrection announcement is, for Marcus, the Gospel's most personal moment. Peter is the one whose denial was most explicit (14:66-72); the one most specifically warned (14:30); the one who "broke down and wept" (14:72). His specific inclusion in the first resurrection message is the Gospel's enacted statement of grace: the most conspicuous failure is the most specifically sought. Marcus reads this as confirming the Gospel's Petrine origin: Peter would not have preserved the denial without the restoration.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: The empty tomb scene characterizes the women through action and speech: they buy spices, they walk at dawn, they ask about the stone, they enter, they see, they flee. The narrator gives them speech (15:47: two Mary's watch where he was buried; 16:3: who will roll the stone?) but no interiority until the final verse: "trembling and bewildered... they said nothing to anyone, because they were afraid." Rhoads argues that the women's silence mirrors and reverses the disciples' flight at 14:50 — not abandonment but paralysis before the overwhelming event.

Rhoads: "They said nothing to anyone, because they were afraid" — the most discussed ending in the NT. Rhoads argues that 16:8 is the intended ending: it is consistent with Mark's narrative technique of withheld resolution, consistent with the women's characterization (fear, silence), and consistent with the Gospel's opening gambit (the reader knows what the characters do not). The Gospel's silence is the reader's invitation: the story does not resolve — it opens. The reader is the next character who must decide what to do with the empty tomb.

The women's visit to the tomb is, for Chrysostom, the faithful remnant: they were last at the cross and first at the resurrection. The practical concern about the stone (v3: "Who will roll the stone away?") is, for Origen, the figure of every human obstacle to the resurrection faith — the stone is already rolled back before they arrive. The young man in white is, for Bede, an angel — Mark's restraint in not naming the figure is itself significant. The command to tell the disciples "and Peter" is, for Chrysostom, the pastoral heart of the resurrection announcement: Peter is named specifically so he will know he is not excluded despite his denial.

The ending of Mark at verse 8 — "they said nothing to anyone, for they were afraid" — is, for Origen and Chrysostom alike in the Catena tradition, not a deficient ending but a deliberately open one. The fear is the human response to the shattering of all categories; the silence is temporary, not permanent (the Gospel exists as proof that the silence was eventually broken). Bede says the abrupt ending is Mark's final characteristic: all his endings are sudden, driving the reader forward. The resurrection is announced, the mission is commissioned, and the reader is left to be the continuation of the story.
He has risen! He is not here — the resurrection is announced by location: not here. The tomb is evidence by its emptiness. The specific “see the place where they laid him” is an invitation to verify.

Go, tell his disciples and Peter — the specific naming of Peter is the most tender detail in the resurrection announcement. Peter, who denied three times (14:66-72), is specifically included. The one who most needed restoration is specifically named.

They said nothing to anyone, because they were afraid — Calvin: the women’s silence is immediately temporary; the appearance of the risen Jesus to them (as Matt and John record) overcomes the fear. Mark’s ending anticipates what will happen; it does not describe it. The silence is the gap that the reader’s own encounter with the risen Christ must fill.

He is going ahead of you into Galilee — Calvin: the return to Galilee reverses the journey of the Passion. The disciples were scattered (14:50); the risen Lord reassembles them in Galilee where they were first called. The resurrection is a new beginning, not merely a restoration.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The original ending of Mark — the vast majority of textual scholars regard 16:8 as the original ending of Mark. The abrupt close (gar endings in Greek — “they were afraid, for...”) is linguistically unusual but not impossible. The Gospel’s characteristic “euthys” urgency carries into the ending: the story is still moving.

And Peter — the two-word addition singles out Peter from the group. The angel’s message includes him specifically. This anticipates Peter’s restoration (alluded to in John 21) and his central role in the early church. Mark’s Gospel, traditionally the voice of Peter’s preaching, ends with the risen Christ seeking Peter out.

hist.context: Mark’s Gospel ends at 16:8 in the oldest and most reliable manuscripts: the women flee, trembling and afraid, and say nothing to anyone. This abrupt ending has puzzled readers since antiquity — hence the later addition of vv.9-20. But the ending is profoundly Markan: the Gospel began with an announcement (“the beginning of the good news”) and ends by throwing the story forward. The reader, like the women, is left with the empty tomb and the commission to go to Galilee.

sections.header: Verses 9–20 — Post-Resurrection Appearances; The Great Commission [Longer Ending]

heb.paragraph: Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved — the longer ending’s commission is built on pisteuō. The Great Commission in this form connects faith, baptism, salvation, and signs. The signs that accompany belief echo the signs of the Galilean ministry: exorcism, new tongues, healing.

tl.name: Easter and beyond — Appearances; Ascension
tl.text: Appearances to Mary Magdalene, two on the road, and the Eleven. The Great Commission given. The Lord taken up into heaven.

notes.note: Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved — Calvin: faith is the condition; baptism is the sign that confirms and seals it. The verse does not make baptism a second condition of salvation alongside faith but joins the sign to the reality it represents.

He was taken up into heaven and sat at the right hand of God. Then the disciples went out and preached everywhere — Calvin: the Ascension is the precondition of the mission. The Lord who goes to the right hand of God is precisely the one who then works with his disciples throughout the world.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The shorter ending of Mark (only known in a few manuscripts) and the longer ending (16:9-20) are both secondary additions. The longer ending synthesizes material from Matt 28, Luke 24, John 20, and Acts. It became canonical in the Western church and is included in the NT canon, but it is not part of the original Markan text.

He was taken up into heaven — the Ascension is the expected conclusion to the resurrection narrative. The longer ending places Jesus at the right hand of God, fulfilling the Ps 110:1 quotation in 12:36 and the trial declaration in 14:62.

mar.source: Joel Marcus,Mark 1–8; Mark 8–16(Word Biblical Commentary) — Faithful Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus treats the longer ending of Mark (vv.9-20) with careful historical caution: the manuscript evidence strongly suggests it was not original to Mark's Gospel but was added by a second-century editor drawing on the other Gospels. Nonetheless, it entered the canon early and was widely read as Scripture in the patristic period. The pattern of the appearances — Mary first, then two disciples, then the Eleven — mirrors Luke and John. The repeated theme of unbelief (vv.11, 13, 14) is the longer ending's theological contribution: the resurrection was not self-evident even to those who loved Jesus.

The mission commission in vv.15-16 is, for Marcus, a post-Easter expansion of the Galilean mission (6:7-13): the scope is now universal and the authority is now fully disclosed. The confirming signs (vv.17-18) represent the charismatic dimension of early Christian mission as documented in Acts and the Pauline letters. Marcus notes that the ascension in v19 is the most compressed in the canon — no cloud, no disciples watching, just the simple statement of session at the right hand. The final verse "the Lord worked with them" is for Marcus the theological thesis of Acts, summarised in a single sentence.

The appearances in the longer ending are, for Chrysostom, a progressive restoration of the scattered community: first to Mary Magdalene (the individual), then to two disciples on the road, then to the Eleven. The repeated unbelief of those who heard the testimony — they did not believe Mary, they did not believe the two — is, for Origen, not a rebuke of the early witnesses but a record of what faith required: these disciples had to be confronted personally before they believed. The rebuke in v14 ("He rebuked their stubborn refusal to believe") is, for Bede, the last correction before the commission.

The Great Commission in Mark is the broadest in scope: "all creation." Chrysostom notes that this exceeds even Matthew's "all nations" — the Markan commission is cosmic. The confirming signs are, for Ambrose, not conditions for salvation but evidences accompanying the proclamation in the apostolic era. The ascension is described with characteristic Markan economy: he was taken up, he sat at God's right hand, they went out. Bede says the final verse — "the Lord worked with them" — is Mark's last and most important word: the Gospel does not end with the ascension but with the continuing partnership between the exalted Christ and his sent people.

ppl.name: Mary Magdalene
ppl.role: First witness to the resurrection; the one out of whom seven demons had been driven
ppl.text: She is named three times in the burial and resurrection narrative (15:40, 47; 16:1, 9). The first witness to the resurrection is one who had been most dramatically delivered by the risen Lord. Her testimony is the Gospel’s first post-resurrection report.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Don't be alarmed," he said. "You are looking for Jesus the Nazarene, who was crucified. He has risen! He is not here. See the place where they laid him."ESV"Do not be alarmed. You seek Jesus of Nazareth, who was crucified. He has risen; he is not here. See the place where they laid him."KJV"Be not affrighted: Ye seek Jesus of Nazareth, which was crucified: he is risen; he is not here: behold the place where they laid him."NASB"Do not be alarmed; you are looking for Jesus the Nazarene, who has been crucified. He has risen; He is not here; look, here is the place where they laid Him."NLT"Don't be alarmed," he said. "You are looking for Jesus of Nazareth, who was crucified. He isn't here! He is risen from the dead! Look, this is where they laid his body."

src.title: Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus (4th century)
src.quote: The two most complete and oldest surviving Greek NT manuscripts both end Mark at 16:8 with a scribal note that the ending was disputed in the manuscripts known to them.
src.note: The most important external evidence that 16:1-8 is the original ending of Mark.

src.title: Eusebius, Quaestiones ad Marinum (c.AD 300)
src.quote: Eusebius explicitly states that accurate copies of Mark known to him ended at 16:8 and that the longer ending was absent from almost all manuscripts.
src.note: The earliest explicit textual commentary on the ending question; confirms the scholarly consensus.

rec.who: N.T. Wright, The Resurrection of the Son of God (2003)
rec.text: Wright argued that Mark’s original ending at 16:8 is deliberate: the Gospel ends where new creation begins. The women’s fear and silence are the appropriate response to the eruption of God’s new world into the old. The reader is left to respond in the women’s place.

rec.who: Richard Bauckham, Jesus and the Eyewitnesses (2006)
rec.text: Bauckham argued that Mark’s Gospel is grounded in Peter’s eyewitness testimony, and that the specific mention of Peter in 16:7 is the Gospel’s final personal touch: the one whose testimony underlies the whole narrative is specifically invited to the resurrection encounter that will restore him.

rows.label: The Gospel’s Structure: Beginning and Non-Ending

hebtext.note: He has risen — ēgerthē: the divine passive. God raised Jesus from the dead. The passive voice is the NT’s consistent grammar for the resurrection: not that Jesus raised himself but that the Father raised the Son (Acts 2:24; Rom 8:11). The resurrection is the Father’s vindication of the Son.

Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved — the longer ending’s commission is built on pisteuō. The Great Commission in this form connects faith, baptism, salvation, and signs. The signs that accompany belief echo the signs of the Galilean ministry: exorcism, new tongues, healing.

notes.title: The original ending — ending at ephobonto gar
notes.content: Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, and the majority of early MSSend at 16:8 with ephobonto gar ("for they were afraid").Later MSSadd the Longer Ending (16:9-20); a few add the Shorter Ending.
notes.note: The Longer Ending (16:9-20) is absent from the two oldest complete Greek MSS, was unknown to Clement and Origen, and is noted as absent or disputed by Eusebius and Jerome. Most textual critics regard 16:8 as the original ending of Mark, whether deliberately abrupt or accidentally truncated.

notes.title: The Longer Ending — textual status
notes.content: Irenaeus (c.AD 185)is the earliest patristic witness to cite from the Longer Ending, confirming it circulated by the late second century. The vocabulary and style differ measurably from 1:1-16:8.
notes.note: The Longer Ending is canonical (included in the NT canon since the fourth century) but not original to Mark. It is a second-century synthesis drawing on resurrection traditions from Matthew, Luke, John, and Acts. Its canonical status does not depend on Markan authorship.

stories.title: The Long Ending of Mark
stories.summary: The twelve verses following 16:8 are absent from the two earliest complete manuscripts of the New Testament. The text ends abruptly at 16:8 with the women fleeing in fear — a conclusion that troubled ancient copyists and prompted several supplemental endings.

evidence.reading: Ends at 16:8; blank column left after, suggesting scribe knew of longer ending
States that 16:9-20 is absent from accurate manuscripts known to him
Notes that 16:9-20 is absent from most Greek copies
A one-sentence summary ending appended after 16:8

stories.consensus: The critical consensus holds that Mark originally ended at 16:8 and that the Long Ending (16:9-20) was added in the second century. The vocabulary, style, and content differ from Mark's established patterns. Most modern translations include it with a note.
stories.significance: The abrupt ending at 16:8 may be intentional — Markan irony leaving the reader to complete the story. Alternatively, the original ending may have been lost. Either way, 16:9-20, though not original to Mark, preserves early Christian tradition about post-resurrection appearances.

debate.title: Where does the original Mark end?

positions.name: 16:8 original ending
positions.proponents: The oldest manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus), the earliest patristic witnesses (Clement, Origen, Eusebius), and internal stylistic evidence all point to 16:8 as the original ending. The abrupt ending is Markan in character.

positions.name: 16:9-20 canonical ending
positions.proponents: The longer ending is included in the NT canon (Irenaeus quotes it as early as c.AD 185) and has been the church’s received text for most of Christian history.

themes.note: Mark 16 is the resurrection chapter: the empty tomb, the angel’s announcement, and the commission. The Gospel that began with “the beginning of the good news” ends not with a conclusion but with a continuing mission.

coaching.tip: 'He has risen! He is not here' (v. 6). The women came to anoint a corpse; they find an empty tomb. Their reaction: 'trembling and bewildered... afraid' (v. 8). Resurrection isn't comfortable—it's terrifying.
Mark's abrupt ending (v. 8) leaves readers in suspense. The women flee, saying nothing. We know they eventually spoke—the Gospel exists. Mark's silence invites our response: will we tell?
